coredata.dc:creator: Akhila, Murali Jaya
Subodh, Ganesanpotti
Sibi, K Solaman
Biju, V.
coredata.dc:description: One of the aesthetic concerns of the scientific community is to ensure all people have access to safe drinking water. Sulfide ion is considered as a public health risk-worthy constituent in water. The present work investigates the effect of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of silver nanoparticles (AgNP) on the photoluminescence response of folic acid-derived carbon nanodots (CD) and their application towards sulfide ion detection. Herein, CD are grown on the surface of AgNP, causing an absorption band centered at ∼ 400 nm due to LSPR. AgNP effectively quench the luminescence of CD through the static quenching mechanism. The Ag-CD system is designed as a simple and selective sensor for sulfide ions in water in a linear range of 0.8–1.5 µM with a Limit of Detection (LOD) of 50 nM and a Limit of Quantification (LOQ) of 170 nM. The ion selectivity for sensing was carried out in accordance with the Hard and Soft – Acids and Bases theory and its predictions. The practical applicability of the sensor was tested by determining the spiked sulfide ion concentrations in pond water and tap water, which produced an adequate outcome.

head.ce:title: Folic acid-derived luminescent carbon dots: Effect of Ag inclusion and switch ON sensing of sulfide ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Kerala, Kariavattom Campus, Thiruvananthapuram 695581, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, University of Kerala, Kariavattom Campus, Thiruvananthapuram 695581, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Physics, University of Kerala, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot (CD) grown on the surface of AgNP, and the combined system quenches the luminescence, which is retrieved in the presence of sulfide ion.

One of the aesthetic concerns of the scientific community is to ensure all people have access to safe drinking water. Sulfide ion is considered as a public health risk-worthy constituent in water. The present work investigates the effect of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of silver nanoparticles (AgNP) on the photoluminescence response of folic acid-derived carbon nanodots (CD) and their application towards sulfide ion detection. Herein, CD are grown on the surface of AgNP, causing an absorption band centered at ∼ 400 nm due to LSPR. AgNP effectively quench the luminescence of CD through the static quenching mechanism. The Ag-CD system is designed as a simple and selective sensor for sulfide ions in water in a linear range of 0.8–1.5 µM with a Limit of Detection (LOD) of 50 nM and a Limit of Quantification (LOQ) of 170 nM. The ion selectivity for sensing was carried out in accordance with the Hard and Soft – Acids and Bases theory and its predictions. The practical applicability of the sensor was tested by determining the spiked sulfide ion concentrations in pond water and tap water, which produced an adequate outcome.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence-based detection and sensing of various anion and cation species in water is an emerging area of research. For such applications, luminescent materials that exhibit high selectivity, sensitivity, and fast response towards the analyte being probed is required. For practical applications, such systems should be non-toxic, and fast-effective. Luminescent carbon dots (CD) are viewed as a potential candidate for sensing applications due to their suitable optical properties, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis routes, chemical stability, etc.[1–4]. These carbon-based nanomaterials consist of both sp2 and sp3 carbon with associated chemical constituents such as oxygen and nitrogen [5,6]. The luminescent properties of these materials can be enhanced and improved by tuning their surface functional groups [7].

Synthesis of carbon dots include polymerization of precursor, dehydration, carbonization and surface passivation [8]. Past few years have witnessed an immense rise in green synthetic approaches for the production of CD with biomaterials employed as starting materials. Their proven benefits include easy synthesis, low cytotoxicity, superior optical properties, self-passivation etc. [9]. But the complexity in precursor composition leads to heterogeneity in CD, which can be a bottleneck for the reproducibility of results [10]. CD derived from biological sources often show excitation dependent luminescence owing to the heterogeneity of carbon source which result in nonuniform size and nonuniform functionalization. Hence carbon dots have been prepared from chemical precursors which serves as both carbon source, and heteroatom dopant or the passivation agent [7]. Typical carbon sources include citric acid, sodium citrate, acetic acid, p-phenylenediamine, folic acid, etc. [11–20]. Folic acid (C19H19N7O6), in the family of B- vitamins is enriched with functional groups such as –OH, –NH2 and –COOH [13]. Without any additives or passivation agents, folic acid is adequate to produce CD by the condensation, polymerization and carbonization. Hence, folic acid is a good precursor to synthesize CD.

The presence of noble metal nanoparticles such as silver or gold in the vicinity of a carbon dot influences its emission characteristics [21]. The luminescence may be enhanced or quenched according as whether the radiative or nonradiative plasmon resonance take place in these structures [22,23]. The separation between metal nanoparticle and CD strongly influences the emission properties. For large separations, the luminescence intensity is not much affected, while smaller separations enhance the non-radiative relaxation of excited fluorophore, and the energy is coupled into the surface plasmons. This leads to quenching of the emission [24]. However, enhancement in photoluminescence of CD in presence of plasmonic nanoparticles have also been reported. Such fluorescence enhancement occurs in the presence of a spacer in-between the CD and the metal nanoparticle, which is known as 'Metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF)'. In MEF, the fluorescence enhancement is due to the strong interaction or overlap of the local electromagnetic field of excitons in the emissive layer in CD and surface plasmons [21]. Fluorescence enhancement is stronger for few nanometer separation distance between semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoparticles. Chan et al. reported a 10.8 nm distance between CdSe dots and AuNP particles, for 2-fold luminescence enhancement [25]. According to Pompa et al., the luminescence of CdSe/ZnS was enhanced by 30 fold when the distance was 35 nm [26]. If the quenched/enhanced emission of these CD-metal nanoparticle system can be retrieved with an analyte which has affinity towards the noble metal nanoparticles, then the system can be used for sensing applications [24].

Sulfide ions (S2-) emanate out mostly as ionization by-products from H2S and sulfur-containing organic compounds. It accumulates in and around our environment through unscientific and careless waste management methods by commercial industries such as fertilizer outlets, contaminated stagnant water bodies, cosmetic wastes, etc. [27]. A proton succumbed sulfide ion becomes highly toxic and destructive in its action. The sulfide ion concentration above the optimum level results in chronic physiological, biochemical, and psychological issues in human. It is the need of the hour to propose a suitable chemical method to detect the sulfide ion concentration levels in water. Sulfide ion sensing is nowadays being carried out through several methods such as electrochemical detection, titrimetry, chromatography, colorimetry, atomic absorption spectrometry, voltammetry, etc. [28–34]. Over and above all these analytical methods, the luminescent detection technique is rather interesting due to its operational stability, analysis duration, high sensitivity, selectivity, and simple experimental procedures [35–40].

The present work investigates the properties of folic acid-derived CD, focusing on their structural and functional behaviours. The formation of silver-CD (Ag-CD) and observed photoluminescence quenching is studied in detail. The Ag-CD system is successfully employed for the detection of sulfide ion concentrations in water. Compared with previously reported fluorescence-based sulfide ion sensors, the present probe shows low limit of detection (LOD), 50 nM [35–40]. According to World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum safe limit of S2- concentration in drinking water should be less than 50 × 103 nM [35,41]. So, this sensor could be useful for the detection of S2- in real samples with reliability. Importantly, as far as we know, this is the first time silver nanoparticles are combined with folic acid-derived CD for sulfide ion detection. The combination of cdot luminescence and metal plasmonic activity in a single nanoparticle would enable multiapplication simultaneously. Moreover, we have successfully explored the possible mechanism for the quenching and enhancement of photoluminescence in the absence and presence of sulfide ion in detail. The observed luminescence quenching in the Ag-CD system is likely due to static quenching mechanism. We also examine selectivity of the present sensor towards sulfide ion and the performance of Ag-CD system for realizing the practical applicability in real water samples.

Folic acid (C19H19N7O6, MW: 441.40 g/mol, ≥97 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, MW: 159.87 g/mol, ≥99.0 %), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, MW: 37.83 g/mol, ≥ 98.0 %), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, MW: 237.66 g/mol), potassium nitrate (KNO3, MW: 101.1 g/mol), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (CaN2O6·4H2O,MW: 236.15 g/mol), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, MW: 142.04 g/mol), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, MW: 84.007 g/mol), potassium iodide (KI, MW: 166.0028 g/mol), tin fluoride (SnF2, MW: 156.69 g/mol), and magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (MgN2O6·6H2O, MW: 256.41 g/mol) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Sodium sulfide (Na2S, MW: 78.04 g/mol), and potassium bromide (KBr, MW: 119.002 g/mol) were purchased from Merck Specialities, USA, while sodium chloride (NaCl, MW: 58.44 g/mol) was purchased from Nice Chemicals, India. All reagents were used without further purification. Deionized water purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA was used in all experiments.

CD were synthesised via hydrothermal method using folic acid as the precursor. Folic acid (0.01 g) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to obtain a homogenous solution (pH ∼ 5). This solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was maintained at 180 °C for 6 hrs in a hydrothermal furnace. The solution was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain a dark golden yellow coloured CD solution. It was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min and the supernatant was collected and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

As prepared CD solution (10 mL) and NaBH4 solution (75 mL, 0.005 M) were taken in a conical flask. It was stirred in an ice bath with the dropwise addition of AgNO3 solution (25 mL, 0.0025 M). The colour of the solution changed to dark brown, indicating the formation of colloidal silver. The Ag-CD sample was kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis.

The experiment was repeated for different concentrations of AgNO3 and NaBH4. The details are included in Table 1 . There was no colour change for the Ag-CD even after several days. Silver nanoparticles (AgNP) were prepared through a similar procedure in the absence of CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sodium sulfide solution for sulfide ion sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Analyte solutions with sulfide ion concentration in the range 0.8 to 1.5 μM were prepared by dissolving known amount of Na2S in deionized water. To demonstrate the selectivity of the sensing system, NiCl2·6H2O, KNO3, CaN2O6·4H2O, MgN2O6·6H2O, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, KI, SnF2, KBr, and NaCl solutions were prepared in the same concentration range following a similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sodium sulfide solution for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Water collected from Hymavathy Pond, in the University of Kerala Campus and tap water from local supply system were selected as real samples. These samples were filtered two times through qualitative filter paper. Known amount of Na2S was dissolved in these water samples and analyte solutions were prepared in the concentration range 0.8 to 1.5 μM.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the CD sample drop-casted on a glass slide was performed in a Bruker D8 Advance Diffractometer using Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Diffraction patterns were recorded in the 2θ range from 10 to 80° with a step size of 0.02°. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis of CD, Ag-CD 1 and Ag-CD 3 samples were done using an FEI-Tecnai G2, F30 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) operating at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV.

FTIR spectra of the CD and Ag-CD samples were obtained using a JASCO FT/IR 6800 Spectrometer using the KBr pellet method. The spectral range was 400–4000 cm−1 with 4 cm−1 resolution and 50 scans. Raman spectra were recorded using a Horiba Lab RAM HR Evolution Raman Spectrometer with a 785 nm laser excitation source.

Quantitative analysis of the surface elemental composition of CD and Ag-CD 3 samples were carried out in a Thermo Scientific ESCALB XI + A1528 system using Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) as the source. The sample solutions were drop-casted on a silicon wafer for the analysis. Survey scan and high-resolution narrow scan spectra (C 1S, O 1S, N 1S and Ag 3d) of the samples were recorded. UV–Visible absorption spectra of all the samples were recorded in the range 200–700 nm using a JASCO V-750 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded using a Fluoromax 4C Spectrofluorometer using a Xenon lamp source. The excitation varied PL emission spectra were recorded by progressively increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 440 nm with a successive increment of 10 nm. Both excitation and emission were taken using slit width of 2 nm bandpass, and integration time was 0.1 s. PL decay measurements were done in a Horiba Scientific Fluorolog TCSPC with a 350 nm LED excitation. Quanta-φ F-3029 integrating sphere setup attached to the Fluorolog-TCSPC was used for the absolute quantum yield measurement of CD and Ag-CD samples at 380 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of sulfide ion using Ag-CD 3
ce:section.ce:para: PL spectra of Ag-CD 3 were recorded in the presence of different concentrations of sulfide ion at 380 nm excitation wavelength. Sensing solution was prepared by adding Ag-CD 3 (0.5 mL) to a test tube containing 3 mL deionized water. To the above sensing solution, Na2S solution was added, and the mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 10 min. The solution was then transferred to a 4 mL quartz cuvette and the PL spectrum was recorded. The same procedure was followed for different concentrations of sulfide ions. Each set of measurements were repeated four times for ensuring reproducibility and the average was used for the analysis. To study the selectivity of the sensor similar experiments were performed by replacing Na2S solution by different ion solutions viz., NiCl2·6H2O, KNO3, CaN2O6·4H2O, MgN2O6·6H2O, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, KI, SnF2, KBr, and NaCl.

XRD pattern of CD is shown in Fig. 1 (a). It has two broad peaks centered around 29° and 40°, which correspond to (002) and (100) graphite planes, respectively [13]. Broad nature of these peaks indicates poor crystallinity of CD [42]. Generally, the graphite structure shows a (002) peak between 20° and 26°. Here the shift towards higher 2θ values is due to the decrease in the sp2 layer spacing [43]. The interplanar spacing corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes are 0.31 nm and 0.22 nm, respectively. Fig. 1(b) is the HRTEM image of CD featuring the graphitic lattice planes of the carbon core. Particles are nearly spherical, with a narrow size distribution in the range 3–8 nm. Fig. S1(a) shows the TEM histogram of CD and Fig. S1(b) represents the TEM image of CD with higher magnification. The average particle size is ∼ 5 ± 1 nm. The inset of Fig. 1(b) shows well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm close to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon, which agrees with the XRD results [44]. The d-spacing value calculated from the SAED pattern (Fig. S2) is 0.24 nm. The amorphous nature of CD is also evident from the diffused halo in the SAED pattern [44].

Raman spectrum of CD exhibits two distinct bands at 1371 cm−1 and 1890 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The deconvolution of the prominent peak at 1371 cm−1 assuming the Lorentzian profile gives two bands corresponding to the disordered D band at 1371 cm−1 and the crystalline G band at 1447 cm−1. The D band is associated with the sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, and the vibrations of sp2 hybridized ones contribute to the G band [45]. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) measures the extent of disorder in carbon structures. The ID/IG value is ∼ 2, which indicates the presence of both sp3 and sp2 defects in CD. This is in agreement with the XRD result [42]. Also, the presence of a strong D band indicates the predominance of CC single bond in CD, which is connected to functional groups on their surface, and is related to the amorphous nature of CD [42]. Also, the broad D band reveals that the bands originate from the amorphous carbon [46]. Raman band around 1800 cm−1 is associated with the N-heterocycles and phenyl groups in folic acid, indicating that these principle moieties are retained in the CD [47].

In the FTIR spectrum of CD shown in Fig. 2(b), the major absorbance bands present are 3415 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration), 3174 cm−1 (NH2 stretching), 2933/2857 cm−1 (CH stretching), 1700 cm−1 (CO/CN stretching), 1389 cm−1 (NH bimodal stretching), 1295 cm−1 (OH in-plane bending vibration) and 1019 cm−1 (COC stretching) [47,48]. The hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups improve the solubility of CD in water [49]. Presence of these functional groups result in the poor crystallinity of CD, which agrees with the XRD result [42].

XPS survey spectrum of CD (Fig. 3 (a)) shows three distinct peaks of C 1S (284.90 eV), O 1S (532.78 eV), and N 1S (397.75 eV) with 41.51, 53.03, and 5.45 atomic percentages, respectively. The C 1S narrow scan spectrum shown in Fig. 3(b) is fitted using five components centered at 283.27, 284.60, 285.08, 286.54, and 288.97 eV assigned to CC, CC, CN, COH, and CO/OCO groups with 17.54, 33.30, 29.82, 11.05, and 1.62 atomic percentages, respectively [50,51]. The predominance of the CC peak in the C 1S spectrum is in agreement with the intense D band in the Raman spectrum. The O1S scan in Fig. 3(c) is deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to the CO bond at 531.80 eV, OCOH bond at 532.47 eV, and OH bond at 533.05 eV. Similarly, the peaks located at 396.71, 397.20, and 398.01 eV in the N 1S spectra (Fig. 3(d)) are attributed to the pyridine-like N, graphitic-N, and amino N, respectively [47].

From the structural analysis, it is inferred that the synthesised CD is dominantly amorphous with poorly crystalline graphitic regions. The broad XRD peaks and predominant CC single bond connected with surface functional groups in the Raman and XPS confirms the dominant amorphous nature. The presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups is confirmed from the FTIR spectrum.

UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CD (Fig. 4 (a)) shows three peaks centered at 235, 280, and 330 nm. The π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains in the CC/C-N bond contributes to the first peak, while the latter two originate from the n-π* transition of the CO bond [52,53].

Fig. 4(b) presents the excitation varied PL emission spectra of CD. The increase of the excitation wavelength from 300 to 380 nm strengthens the PL emission with negligible shift in the peak position (Fig. S3). This indicates excitation-independent emission which is in agreement with previous reports on folic acid-derived carbon dots [13]. The maximum intense emission of this broad PL band appears ∼ 455 nm upon 380 nm excitation. Further increase in the excitation wavelength from 380 to 440 nm shows a notable redshift in the emission wavelength and weakening of PL intensity (Fig. S3 and Fig. 4(c)). Even though, the origin of fluorescence in carbon dots still remains a subject matter of debate, studies points to three main possibilities: (i) core state emission, (ii) surface state emission, which is determined by the hybridization of carbon backbone and the connected chemical groups, and (iii) molecular fluorescence, which is induced by the fluorescent impurities generated during the synthesis [54]. Excitation-dependent emission could be attributed to the existence of multiple emission centers and excitation-independent emission is due to the homogeneity in carbon dot’s structure [55]. Here, TEM histograms shown in Fig. S1 indicate that CD are not uniform in size. Therefore, the photoluminescence emission of CD depends on the surface states rather than the homogeneity of core. Also, the absorption at 235 nm (π-π*) produces nearly no photoluminescence (Fig. S4). It also suggests that the observed photoluminescence is not from the electronic conjugate structures and the surface states are responsible for the emission [44].

In the present case, the higher energy irradiation on CD can excite the dominant sp3 hybridized (large energy gap) carbon domains, resulting in short-wavelength emission and hence does not affect the position and shape of the photoluminescence spectrum [5]. While the irradiation of higher wavelength (low energy) excites the less abundant, sp2 hybridized (small energy gap) domains results in the emission of longer wavelength and weakening of signal (Fig. 4(b) & (c)).

The absolute luminescence quantum yield is found to be 7.98 % at 380 nm excitation. The PL decay curve of CD is shown in Fig. 4(d). A triple exponential function that perfectly fit the emission decay indicates that CD possess three emissive states [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of Ag-CD sample
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the TEM images of Ag-CD 1 and Ag-CD 3. The successive addition of AgNO3 in CD shows an increase in the average size. Ag-CD 1 has an average size of 8 ± 2 nm whereas Ag-CD 3 has 23 ± 4 nm. The TEM images confirmed the presence of distribution of AgNP on the surface of CD. Due to the low contrast of CD on the substrate, the CD are hardly seen in the image [58]. The lattice spacing values for Ag-CD 1 (0.14 nm) and Ag-CD 3 (0.12 nm) corresponds to the (220) and (311) planes of AgNP [59]. The Silver nanoparticles appear darker in the micrographs as the atomic number of silver is more than that of carbon atoms.

Fig. 6 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum and high-resolution narrow scan spectra of Ag-CD 3. The survey scan reveals carbon (C 1S) and oxygen (O 1S) at 284.80 and 532.25 eV with 54.49 % and 43.14 %, respectively. Presence of N 1S (399.93 eV) and Ag 3d (367.82 eV and 373.83 eV) is notable yet feeble with 2 %, and 0.47 %, respectively.

High resolution C 1S spectrum in Fig. 6(b) is deconvoluted into five peaks. They are assigned to CC (284.21 eV), CC (284.82 eV), CN (285.44 eV), COH (286.85 eV), and CO/OCO (288.11 eV). The peaks located at 531.87, 532.72, and 533.4 eV in the O 1S spectra (Fig. 6(c)) are attributed to CO, OCOH, and OH bonds. There is an electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged functional groups of the CD and the silver ions [60]. Due to this, the oxygen-containing surface functional groups of CD might contribute to reducing silver ions. It results in a drastic change in the O 1S/C 1S ratio of Ag-CD compared with that of CD.

In the C 1S spectrum, the intensity of C-OH bond was decreased and CO group was increased. Compared with the O 1S spectrum of CD, the OH groups become more intense than OCOH group. It indicated that the carboxyl groups were reduced by the reducing agent, NaBH4 to alcohol and thereby its intensity increased [61]. The binding energies of 367.82 eV and 373.83 eV in the Ag 3d spectra (Fig. 6 (d)) are assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2. Here the difference between the two peaks is ∼ 6.01 eV, which indicates that the Ag exists as zero-valent Ag nanoparticles in the Ag-CD system [62].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the influence of AgNP on the absorption spectrum of CD. The characteristic property of AgNP is the presence of a localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) band in the visible region of absorption spectrum, which exhibits a blueshift or redshift depending on the parameters such as particle size, shape, aggregation, etc. [63]. The as-prepared colloidal solution of the Ag-CD samples show an absorption band centered at ∼ 400 nm due to the LSPR while, CD possess only the three electronic transitions (π-π* and n-π*), as already discussed [64]. This confirms the successful inclusion of Ag in the CD solution. The absorbance spectra of Ag-CD 3 recorded after 3 months and 7 months of preparation shows LSPR band which indicates that the Ag-CD systems are stable (Fig. S5). The latter one shows a small blueshift in the SPR band, due to the sedimentation of larger particles on aggregation [65].

Fig. 7 (b) shows the PL emission spectra of CD and Ag-CD systems recorded with an excitation of 380 nm, which corresponds to the maximum emission of CD. The PL intensity is quenched with the increase in the concentration of silver. In Fig. 7(b) intensity is plotted in log scale. Fig. S6 shows the PL spectra of CD and AgNP. Previous reports have shown that the combination of luminescence of carbon dots with metal plasmonic activity can either enhance or quench the luminescence of CD [56]. The luminescence quenching activity was observed for the direct attachment of CD to metal plasmonic nanoparticles [56]. The possible mechanisms of quenching include static quenching, dynamic quenching, inner filter effect (IFE), and förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [24,66–68]. Here, the emission intensity of Ag-CD was significantly quenched. The Ag-CD 1, Ag-CD 2, and Ag-CD 3 systems have 6.99, 6.51, and 4.54 % PL quantum yield, respectively. From the previous discussion, it is clear that the surface functional groups in CD contribute for photoluminescence [54]. When the Ag-CD system is formed, the CD get directly attached to silver.

As shown in Fig. 7(c), the excitation spectrum of CD overlaps with the absorption of AgNP. Also, there is a slight overlap between the absorption of AgNP with the emission of CD. This causes a reduction in the excitation efficiency of cdot. It is observed that, when Ag nanoparticles and CD was physically mixed, no appreciable quenching in PL intensity occurs (Fig. S7). Hence, the possibility of inner filter effect can rule out. The dynamic quenching results due to the collision driven energy transfer between CD in the excited state and quencher in the ground state. Hence, dynamic quenching is not a probable mechanism of quenching. FRET mechanism is reported to resulting an appreciable decrease in fluorescence decay time [68]. Hence, the possibility of FRET mechanism can also rule out. With the available data, the most probable mechanism of photoluminescence quenching in Ag-CD system is static quenching due to strong interaction between surface states of CD and AgNP leading to the formation of a complex. No appreciable change in the decay time (Table S1) and the presence of absorption peaks of CD and AgNP in the UV–Vis absorption supports this proposal [68].

The influence of NaBH4 precursor alone on the photoluminescence emission of CD was also studied. In this case, PL emission of CD was not much affected (Fig. S8). This result confirms that the addition of NaBH4 does not contribute to the PL quenching in the Ag-CD system. Moreover, the PL emission spectrum of the CD-AgNP system, in which both the constituents are simply mixed, is recorded. But no quenching of luminescence was observed in this case (Fig. S7). It also confirms that specific stirring conditions such as ice-bath, stirring time, addition of reagents, etc. depends the formation of Ag-CD system.

In addition to the PL intensity, the influence of silver plasmonic activity on the spectroscopic parameters such as quantum yield and luminescence decay time has been investigated (Table S1). As expected, the quantum yield value decreased for the Ag-CD system from 7.98 % to 4.54 %. The decay dynamics are shown in Fig. 7(d) and Table S1. Like CD, Ag-CD have two long-lived and one short-lived lifetime component. When Ag-CD systems are formed, the attachment of CD with silver results in the masking of surface functional groups of CD and thereby changing the excited state properties. This leads the Ag-CD fluorophore to spend more time (τ1 component) in a less abundant energy level (Table S1). It is clear that, in the case of Ag-CD, the radiative recombination rate decrease, and hence the radiative lifetime is greater than that of unmodified CD [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulfide ion sensing using Ag-CD 3 as a luminescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Ag-CD 3 sample was chosen as a representative one for sulfide ion sensing since it contains greater amount of silver nitrate in it. Absorbance spectra of Ag-CD 3 were recorded in presence of sulfide ions. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the absorbance maxima of the SPR band decrease gradually as the sulfide ion concentration increase. This implies an interaction between the metallic silver and the added sulfide ion.

Fig. 8(b) presents the photoluminescence spectra of CD, Ag-CD 3, CD/sulfide, and Ag-CD/sulfide under the same experimental conditions. PL intensity of CD is quenched when AgNP are added while it was not at all affected by the addition of sulfide ion. However, the PL intensity of Ag-CD 3 system is increased significantly in presence of sulfide ion. Fig. 8(c) shows the photoluminescence response of the Ag-CD 3 system with different concentrations of sulfide ions. The emission intensity is enhanced after the addition of a 0.8 µM concentration of sulfide ion to the Ag-CD 3 system. The PL intensity increased gradually with a further increase in the sulfide ion concentration from 0.9 to 1.5 µM.

The changes in PL intensity of Ag-CD 3 exhibited a linear relationship with the sulfide ion concentration from 0.8 to 1.5 µM. The intensity variation is presented as plots of
F -
F 0
F 0
vs. concentration [Q], where F0 and F are the photoluminescence intensities of Ag-CD 3 in the absence and presence of sulfide ion. The linear relation of the plot which fits the data is
y = 5.0 x - 2.3
(Fig. 8(d)). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by
3 σ
S
rule (σ is the standard deviation of the response curve and S is the slope of the calibration curve), as 50 ± 1 nM [69]. The calculated LOD is about 300 times smaller than the maximum level of H2S concentration (50x103 nM) recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO) for drinking water [35,41]. Table 2 compares the LOD of reported sulfide ion sensors with the present work. It is seen that the proposed sensor has better limit of detection value compared with reported sensors. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was estimated by
10 σ
S
rule, as 170 ± 4 nM.

Even though silver is an unreactive metal due to its completely filled 4d subshell, it readily reacts with chalcogens to form chalcogenides [70]. Here silver react with sulfide ions to form Ag2S (silver sulfide) in the presence of oxygen, as in equation (3) [71]. Presence of Ag2S is confirmed through the FTIR analysis of the sample containing Ag-CD and sulfide solution (Fig. S9). The FTIR spectrum exhibits the characteristic vibration of Ag-S bond in the 500–600 cm−1 region [72].

According to Pearson’s Hard and Soft – Acids and Bases (HSAB) concept, hard acids will prefer to bind with hard bases, and soft acids prefer to bind with soft bases to form stable compounds. Soft acids are mostly heavy metal ions, characterized by larger size, zero or low positive oxidation state, nearly completely filled d-electrons, and easily polarizable. Soft bases are anions with low electronegativity and high polarizability. Since both silver and sulfide ions belong to the soft category, silver (from the Ag-CD system) has a strong affinity for sulfide and forms a covalent bond [73]. Thus unleashes the surface functional moieties of CD. Hence the luminescence emission is enhanced.

The strength of interaction between Ag-CD 3 and the sulfide ions can be investigated by calculating the binding constant and the number of binding sites. The binding constant (KA) between the AgNP and sulfide ions have been calculated using the equation (4),
(4)

A plot of

vs. log concentration [Q] (Fig. S10) gives a straight line whose slope equals 'n' (the number of binding sites), and the Y-axis intercept equals log KA. In the present study, the high value of the binding constant (KA = 4.3 × 1010 M) illustrates a strong interaction between AgNP and the sulfide ions [79].

As already discussed, silver can form chemical compounds with halogens just like the chalcogens. We examined the sensitivity of Ag-CD towards NaCl [70]. But it is observed that the addition of chloride ions does not significantly affect the emission intensity (Fig. 9 ). Though chlorine has more electronegativity, and both the chloride and sulfide ions have the similar electronic arrangement, the latter has a smaller nuclear charge than chloride and is larger in size. Moreover, chloride ion is a hard base [73]. So, these factors may account for the interaction with silver in the Ag-CD system, which in turn influences the sensing capability.

To evaluate the selectivity of Ag-CD towards sulfide ions, the changes in luminescence intensity of Ag-CD 3 in the presence of different ions and norfloxacin as a representative from antibiotics were studied under the same experimental conditions. The most abundant anions present in water are bicarbonates, chlorides and sulfides, while nitrates, and fluorides are secondary constituents [80]. Fig. 9 shows the luminescence response of Ag-CD 3 with different analytes. The quantity F/F0 represents the luminescence intensity of Ag-CD 3 in the absence and presence of ions. It is seen that only the addition of sulfide ion causes significant changes in the PL intensity of Ag-CD. This indicates the selectivity of the proposed Ag-CD sensor towards sulfide ions.

To demonstrate the practical utility of the present sensor, Ag-CD 3 system was applied to determine the presence of sulfide ion in real water samples. Table 3 shows the percent recovery of the samples in pond water and tap water. Recovery percentage were calculated using the following equation,

Standard deviation is obtained from three different measurements. The recovered value of sulfide concentration is lower than the spiked concentration. The oxidation or volatilization during the analysis causes the loss of sulfide ion from the water sample. It results in the low recovery value compared with the previously reported values [76]. The stable recovery values and fast response time authenticate the reliability of Ag-CD system for the practical sensing application towards sulfide ion.

In summary, excitation-independent luminescent carbon dots (CD) were synthesised from folic acid by hydrothermal method with a quantum efficiency of 7.98 %. The as-prepared CD is combined with AgNP to form Ag-CD system using a facile magnetic stirring method. The Ag-CD system show an LSPR band near 400 nm in the absorption spectrum, which confirms the successful combination of CD with AgNP. The AgNP addition on the CD quenches the PL emission of CD through the static quenching mechanism. We employed this property of Ag-CD system as a luminescent probe for the sulfide ion sensing. The addition of 0.8 µM sulfide ions to Ag-CD decreases the LSPR absorption band and increases the emission intensity. This switch-ON luminescent knob showed good selectivity towards sulfide ion with excellent repeatability and reproducibility in the linear range of 0.8–1.5 µM. The limit of detection is obtained as 50 ± 1 nM, which is sufficiently lower than the maximum limit of sulfide ion concentration recommended by WHO and is better than the reported values. The practical application was demonstrated by determining the spiked sulfide ions in pond water and tap water.
